narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 46% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction stemmed primarily from your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and stood out as the strongest signal pushing the model toward forecasting bar exam failure, especially when compared to the much higher average deciles around the sixth for students who succeeded. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.60 is actually stronger than the typical 3.24 seen among those who passed, it worked in your favor by pulling the prediction back toward success, counterbalancing some concerns. Similarly, your LSAT score of 33 provided a moderate positive nudge toward passing, though it fell short of the 37.27 average for successful bar takers. Your middle family income bracket aligns with over a third of those who passed, and your choice of full-time study matches the vast majority of successful students, but these played smaller roles in the model's thinking. Overall, your Year 3 performance was the dominant factor tipping the scales, far outweighing the strengths in your undergrad record and LSAT. As a full-time student from a middle-income background, you share common ground with many who make it through, but the model saw your later academic decile as a red flag not easily offset. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's a data-driven risk assessment to ensure we admit those with the highest predicted odds of bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the successful average, strengthening your Year 3 record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with updated credentials. You might also explore targeted bar prep resources early to build confidence. Many students in similar positions have improved their profiles and succeeded—your solid undergrad GPA shows you have the foundation to do the same. Keep pushing; your path to law school is still very much within reach.
